metadata.title: The Cost of the Pivot
metadata.segment: How Aditi Deodhar Learned to Stop, Discard What Works, and Build What Matters
metadata.audience: Humanitarians AI Fellows — engineers and researchers learning what it costs to build what matters
metadata.greeting_note: Hei = Norwegian for Hello. Liam two-word budget (one word here).
metadata.source_title: The Cost of the Pivot — How Aditi Deodhar Learned to Stop, Discard What Works, and Build What Matters
metadata.note: STUDENT/FELLOW PROFILE mode. Subject: Aditi Deodhar. ONE IDEA: The willingness to stop — to discard working code six hours into a competition and rebuild around the real problem — is a learned discipline, not a personality trait. Liam narrates ABOUT Aditi, never AS her. @HumanitariansAI folderLabel. Only statistic on screen: GPA 3.717 (from article). Direct quote verbatim: 'I\'m not done figuring it all out. Miles to go. But I feel like I\'m getting there.' Public-presence labels only (Google Scholar / Medium / GitHub — no invented URLs). HONESTY: no invented metrics, no invented handles.

beats.narration_text: Profile, Aditi Deodhar. Six hours into the hackathon, her app worked. Then a user asked one question — and she told her team to throw the working code away. Here's why that was the right call. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: Six hours into a hackathon, a working app — then one user question rewrites everything. What is the discipline of knowing when to stop?
props.runningText: profiling an engineer who stops when it matters…
output: The pivot: a discipline, not a personality trait
Building what matters vs. building what works
The zero-budget stack that got her hired
The cost she kept choosing to pay

beats.narration_text: Picture the six-hour mark. A symptom tracker, clean demo, on track to finish. Working code, real progress. Then a user leans in: 'How are women supposed to afford this?' That question names the gap between building what works and building what matters. Aditi stopped the team. Discarded the build. Started over. The decision didn't come from nowhere — twenty-two years in Pune, an Electronics and Telecom degree at PICT, two years as an SDE at Persistent Systems, then a permanent restart in Boston with no network and no scaffolding. She chose the discomfort deliberately. What it built in her was attention to what people navigating unfamiliar systems actually need.

The clearest evidence of that attention: MediPedia. A local RAG medical-FAQ chatbot built on a budget of zero. Ollama running LLMs locally. FAISS vector database. Hugging Face embeddings. Python and Streamlit, every component on her own machine — no paid APIs, no cloud. The constraint wasn't the problem. The constraint was the design brief. The stack is replicable by anyone who can't afford proprietary infrastructure. Jutly Inc. hired her as an AI Engineer on the strength of it.

MediPedia is one chip on a pattern that runs across everything she builds. SecureStream AI: real-time privacy detection on Confluent Cloud, Kafka, Flink, MongoDB — second place at Confluent AI Day 2025, built in a three-hour sprint. MIT Women's Health AI Hackathon: a conversational agent for documented gaps in women's-health research data — gaps in the research itself, not just the tools. FinFluent: an AI personal-finance assistant that helps women navigate healthcare financial decisions — HSAs, deductibles, maternity leave — in natural language. Four builds. Each starts the same way: a gap named, a gap the old tools missed, then something built to serve the people the old tools left out.

props.sparkLine: Gap named. Build. Serves who it missed.

beats.narration_text: In parallel, she was building the room that sixty-plus women in tech needed to exist. Hub Leader for Rewriting the Code Boston: programming over three months. Two students mentored through AnitaB.org on the international-student job hunt. The GHC 2024 Advancing Inclusion Scholarship. Community infrastructure is invisible until it's absent. That's the sentence. You don't notice the work until the network frays. This is the engineering that doesn't make the portfolio slide but makes every other thing possible.

The official number: GPA 3.717. MS in Information Systems, Northeastern, December 2025. The number is real. Here's what it was held alongside: a full co-op at an early-stage Cambridge startup. Hub Leader of a sixty-person organization. Multiple competitive hackathons. Two AWS certifications — Cloud Practitioner and AI Practitioner — earned independently. A cross-country relocation with no network, no playbook. The GPA understates the achievement. The achievement is everything it was held alongside.

The pivot paid off. FinFluent was the DreamAI 2025 finalist pitch — Aditi led it. Every element traces to something specific: the confidence to rebuild, from MediPedia. The instinct to listen and change course, from Jutly. The drive to build for women, from her own experience navigating systems that weren't designed for her. The maturity to pitch from a strategic course she chose. She knows the distance left. 'I'm not done figuring it all out. Miles to go. But I feel like I'm getting there.' Not performed idealism. The honest language of someone who has been paying costs.

props.sparkLine: The pivot has a cost. She keeps paying it.

beats.narration_text: Aditi Deodhar. MS Information Systems, Northeastern University College of Engineering, December 2025. AI Engineer. DreamAI 2025 finalist — FinFluent, pitch lead. Second place, Confluent AI Day 2025, SecureStream AI. GHC 2024 Advancing Inclusion Scholarship from AnitaB.org. Northeastern College of Engineering spotlight, October 2025. She maintains a Google Scholar profile, a Medium technical blog, and an active public GitHub. Article by NortheasternISE, February 27, 2026.

props.sparkLine: Building what matters, not just what works.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The willingness to stop — to discard working code six hours into a competition and rebuild around the real problem — is a learned discipline, not a personality trait. 'This isn't working, let's start over' is the skill that made everything after it possible. Building what matters costs more than building what works. She kept choosing to pay it.

props.artifactTitle: The pivot is a discipline, not a trait.
artifactLines: Stopping is the engineering decision that makes the rest possible.
Constraint is a design problem, not an excuse.
Building what matters costs more than building what works.
The pivot has a cost. She kept choosing to pay it.

beats.narration_text: Here's a prompt to run on your own project. 'I'm building something that technically works. Interview me like a skeptical user: who is this actually for, what can't they afford or access, and where does it works quietly fail the person it's supposed to serve? Then tell me what I'd have to discard to build what matters instead.' Run this before you ship. The question it forces is the one Aditi asked herself six hours in — and the answer is always cheaper to find now than in the field.

props.command: I'm building something that technically works. Interview me like a skeptical user: who is this actually for, what can't they afford or access, and where does 'it works' quietly fail the person it's supposed to serve? Then tell me what I'd have to discard to build what matters instead.

beats.narration_text: The Cost of the Pivot. Liam, in for Bear.
